explanation.text: Bessie cannot see or hear direct instructions, so she watches her duplicate controls move with the instructor's actions. This evidence shows that people learned to fly by carefully observing a pilot.
Bessie plans to open an aviation school where anyone interested can learn. Her goal shows that she wants to share her knowledge of flying with others.

Paragraph 3 describes Bessie's precise final flight test, including an exact landing with the engine off. That preparation connects to paragraph 8 because she later demonstrates those piloting skills before a public crowd.

Three thousand spectators watch Bessie make the first public flight by a Black woman in the United States, and she then flies solo. These historic achievements are the paragraphs' central focus because both paragraphs emphasize her pioneering public flights.

The crowd goes wild after Bessie carries the stuntman, and passengers eagerly line up for rides. These celebratory reactions create a delighted tone because they express the audience's excitement and approval.
Bessie goes to France because flight schools in the United States refuse to train her. France gives her the opportunity to earn a pilot's license that she was denied at home.
Bessie's first public solo flight as a Black woman is the passage's major historical achievement. A summary should emphasize that event because the plane and audience are only supporting details.
The shoes are said to imprison feet that are accustomed to the warm earth. This personification emphasizes Cassie's preference for being barefoot because she associates the outdoors with freedom.
The family's cotton income does not cover the mortgage, taxes, and other expenses, so Papa must work away from home. That financial struggle is the paragraph's main point.
Cassie describes Little Man as extremely neat, Stacey as sour, and Christopher-John as sensitive and cheerful. These contrasting descriptions reveal each sibling's distinct personality and viewpoint.
Cassie recognizes each sibling's different habits and feelings, even explaining why Stacey is in a bad mood. Her accurate descriptions show that the siblings differ but understand one another.

Papa works away from home to help pay the mortgage and taxes, and he promises that the family will not have to live on someone else's land. His actions and words show his determination to keep their land.
Lee objects before learning the complete rules and is immediately dismissed for making a snap judgment. His mistake shows why a wise decision requires having all the available information first.
Persephone studies what each student's choice might mean and realizes that the apparently easy winning answer may be a trap. This careful evaluation shows that she thinks deeply before deciding.
Laura deliberately chooses blades even though the proctor has already revealed stone. Because she accepts a losing position instead of seeking victory, “settle” means to agree to less than one wants.

The examination asks students to select the wisest response, and those who succeed become Deciders. This is the best summary because it captures both the test's purpose and its importance to the students.
Persephone “knew it couldn't be that easy” when the obvious winning choice appeared. That thought reveals her skeptical, analytical perspective on the examination.
Persephone explains that matching stone with stone places both players on an even field, where neither destroys nor bows to the other. Therefore, equality represents fairness.
At first Persephone is unsure whether the examination is chance or a trick. By the end, she understands the choices' meanings, explains the wisdom of equality, and passes.
Each prairie-dog warning has sound differences that people can barely hear, yet the calls change with the kind of danger. Therefore, “distinctive” means having a unique sound.
The recorded calls consistently put “chitter” before “chatter” and never reverse them. That repeated order supports the claim that prairie dogs pay attention to the sequence of sounds.
Prairie dogs make different calls for different shirt colors and even for different types of dogs. Such precise distinctions are surprising evidence of how complex their communication may be.

Paragraph 5 introduces small call differences connected to particular dangers, while paragraph 10 explains that those calls can contain detailed information. The later paragraph develops the meaning of the earlier observation.

Researchers find that one continuous prairie-dog alarm call can identify a threat and describe specific features such as color or type. This evidence supports the idea that a single call carries a great deal of information.
The repeated “chitter-chatter” order suggests that prairie-dog calls follow predictable patterns. The fact that the animals never use the reverse order supports this conclusion.

Spectrograms turn recorded calls into pictures that researchers can slow down and examine. They are important because researchers can compare many alarm calls and notice differences people cannot easily hear.
